From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American traditional folk song popularized in 1964

For other uses, see [The House of the Rising Sun (disambiguation)](/wiki/The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun_(disambiguation) "The House of the Rising Sun (disambiguation)").

"**The House of the Rising Sun**" is an American traditional [folk song](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), sometimes called "**Rising Sun Blues**". It tells of a person's life gone wrong in the city of [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"). Many versions also urge a sibling or parents and children to avoid the same fate. The most successful commercial version, recorded in 1964 by the [English](/wiki/England "England") rock band [The Animals](/wiki/The_Animals "The Animals"), was a number one hit on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") and in the U.S. and Canada.[[1]](#cite_note-the_age-1) As a traditional folk song recorded by an electric rock band, it has been described as the "first [folk rock](/wiki/Folk_rock "Folk rock") hit".[[2]](#cite_note-marsh1001-2)[[3]](#cite_note-mclean-3)

The song was first collected in [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") in the 1930s, but probably has its roots in [traditional English folk song](/wiki/English_folk_music "English folk music").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is listed as [number 6393](/wiki/List_of_folk_songs_by_Roud_number "List of folk songs by Roud number") in the [Roud Folk Song Index](/wiki/Roud_Folk_Song_Index "Roud Folk Song Index").

## Origin and early versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origin and early versions")]

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origin")]

Like many folk songs, "The House of the Rising Sun" is of uncertain authorship. Musicologists say that it is based on the tradition of [broadside ballads](/wiki/Broadside_ballad#Broadside_ballads "Broadside ballad"), and thematically it has some resemblance to the 16th-century ballad "[The Unfortunate Rake](/wiki/The_Unfortunate_Rake "The Unfortunate Rake")" (also cited as source material for "[St. James Infirmary Blues](/wiki/St._James_Infirmary_Blues "St. James Infirmary Blues")"), yet there is no evidence suggesting that there is any direct relation.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The folk song collector [Alan Lomax](/wiki/Alan_Lomax "Alan Lomax") suggested that the melody might be related to a 17th-century folk song, "Lord Barnard and Little Musgrave", also known as "[Matty Groves](/wiki/Matty_Groves "Matty Groves")",[[6]](#cite_note-matteson-6)[[7]](#cite_note-BBCh2g2-7) but a survey by [Bertrand Bronson](/wiki/Bertrand_Harris_Bronson "Bertrand Harris Bronson") showed no clear relationship between the two songs.[[8]](#cite_note-formative-8)

#### Traditional English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Traditional English")]

Lomax also noted that "Rising Sun" was the name of a [bawdy house](/wiki/Bawdy_house "Bawdy house") in two traditional English songs, and a name for English pubs,[[9]](#cite_note-encyclo-9) and proposed that the location of the house was then relocated from England to the U.S. by Southern performers.[[9]](#cite_note-encyclo-9) In 1953, Lomax met [Harry Cox](/wiki/Harry_Cox "Harry Cox"), an English farm labourer known for his impressive folk song repertoire, who knew a song called "She was a Rum One" ([Roud 2128](/wiki/List_of_folk_songs_by_Roud_number "List of folk songs by Roud number")) with two possible opening verses, one beginning

> If you go to [Lowestoft](/wiki/Lowestoft "Lowestoft"), and ask for The Rising Sun,  
> There you'll find two old whores and my old woman is one.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The recording Lomax made of Harry Cox is available online.[[11]](#cite_note-11) (Cox provides the alternative opening verse with the "Rising Sun" line at 1:40 in the recording.) It is considered extremely unlikely that Cox was aware of the American song.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It is also lent credence by the fact that there was a pub in [Lowestoft](/wiki/Lowestoft "Lowestoft") called The Rising Sun and by the fact that the town is the most easterly settlement in the U.K. (hence "rising sun").[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) Doubt has been expressed as to whether Cox's song has any connection to later versions.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: France")]

Folklorist [Vance Randolph](/wiki/Vance_Randolph "Vance Randolph") proposed an alternative origin related to France, in which the "rising sun" might refer to the [sunburst](/wiki/Sunburst "Sunburst") insignia dating to the time of [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), which was brought to [North America by French immigrants](/wiki/French_colonization_of_the_Americas "French colonization of the Americas").[[8]](#cite_note-formative-8)

### Earliest American versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Earliest American versions")]

"House of the Rising Sun" was said to have been known by American miners in 1905.[[6]](#cite_note-matteson-6) The oldest published version of the lyrics is that printed by [Robert Winslow Gordon](/wiki/Robert_Winslow_Gordon "Robert Winslow Gordon") in 1925, in a column titled "Old Songs That Men Have Sung" in *[Adventure](/wiki/Adventure_(magazine) "Adventure (magazine)")* magazine.[[15]](#cite_note-sullivan2013-15) The lyrics of that version begin:[[15]](#cite_note-sullivan2013-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

> There is a house in New Orleans, it's called the Rising Sun  
> It's been the ruin of many poor girl  
> Great God, and I for one.

The oldest known recording of the song, under the title "Rising Sun Blues", is by [Appalachian](/wiki/Appalachian_music "Appalachian music") artists [Clarence "Tom" Ashley](/wiki/Clarence_Ashley "Clarence Ashley") and [Gwen Foster](/wiki/Gwin_Foster "Gwin Foster"), who recorded it on September 6, 1933, on the [Vocalion](/wiki/Vocalion_Records "Vocalion Records") label (02576).[[6]](#cite_note-matteson-6)[[17]](#cite_note-folkwayslinernotes-17) Ashley said he had learned it from his grandfather, Enoch Ashley,[[18]](#cite_note-18) who got married around the time of the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) which suggests that the song could have been written years before the start of the 20th century. [Roy Acuff](/wiki/Roy_Acuff "Roy Acuff"), an "early-day friend and apprentice" of Clarence Ashley's, learned it from him and recorded it as "Rising Sun" on November 3, 1938.[[6]](#cite_note-matteson-6)[[17]](#cite_note-folkwayslinernotes-17)

The narrative of the lyrics has varied between male and female narrators. The earliest known printed version from Gordon's column is about a woman's warning. The earliest known recording of the song by Ashley is about a rounder, a male character. The lyrics of that version begin:[[20]](#cite_note-20)

> There is a house in New Orleans  
> They call the Rising Sun  
> Where many poor boys to destruction has gone  
> And me, oh God, are one.

On an expedition with his wife to eastern [Kentucky](/wiki/Music_of_Kentucky "Music of Kentucky"), the folklorist [Alan Lomax](/wiki/Alan_Lomax "Alan Lomax") set up his recording equipment in [Middlesboro](/wiki/Middlesboro,_Kentucky "Middlesboro, Kentucky"), in the house of the singer and activist Tillman Cadle (husband of [Mary Elizabeth Barnicle](/wiki/Mary_Elizabeth_Barnicle "Mary Elizabeth Barnicle")). There, he recorded a performance by [Georgia Turner](/wiki/Georgia_Turner "Georgia Turner"), the 16-year-old daughter of a local miner. He called it "The Rising Sun Blues".[[17]](#cite_note-folkwayslinernotes-17) Lomax recorded two other different versions in Eastern [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky") in 1937, both of which can be heard online: one sung by Dawson Henson[[21]](#cite_note-21) and another by Bert Martin.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In his 1941 songbook *Our Singing Country*, Lomax credits the song to Georgia Turner, using Martin's extra lyrics to "complete" the song.[[17]](#cite_note-folkwayslinernotes-17)[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Kentucky folk singer [Jean Ritchie](/wiki/Jean_Ritchie "Jean Ritchie") sang a different traditional version of the song to Lomax in 1949, which can be heard online courtesy of the Alan Lomax archive.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Dillard Chandler](/wiki/Dillard_Chandler "Dillard Chandler") of [Madison County](/wiki/Madison_County,_North_Carolina "Madison County, North Carolina"), [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"), sang a variant of the song beginning "There was a sport in New Orleans".[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Several older blues recordings of songs with similar titles are unrelated, for example, "Rising Sun Blues" by Ivy Smith (1927), but Bluesologist for Texas music Coy Prather has argued that "The Risin' Sun" by [Texas Alexander](/wiki/Alger_%22Texas%22_Alexander "Alger \"Texas\" Alexander") (1928) is an early blues version of the hillbilly song.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Ted Anthony in his research on the song noted a lyrical similarity to versions of an old tune called The Rambling Cowboy.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 1941, [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie") recorded a version. [Keynote Records](/wiki/Keynote_Records "Keynote Records") released one by [Josh White](/wiki/Josh_White "Josh White") in 1942,[[28]](#cite_note-keynote-recording-28) and [Decca Records](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records") released one also in 1942 with music by White and the vocals performed by [Libby Holman](/wiki/Libby_Holman "Libby Holman").[[29]](#cite_note-decca-recording-29) Holman and White also collaborated on a 1950 release by [Mercury Records](/wiki/Mercury_Records "Mercury Records"). White is also credited with having written new words and music that have subsequently been popularized in the versions made by many other later artists. White learned the song from a "white hillbilly singer", who might have been Ashley, in [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina") in 1923–1924.[[6]](#cite_note-matteson-6) [Lead Belly](/wiki/Lead_Belly "Lead Belly") recorded two versions of the song, in February 1944 and in October 1948, called "In New Orleans" and "The House of the Rising Sun", respectively; the latter was recorded in sessions that were later used on the album *Lead Belly's Last Sessions* (1953, [Smithsonian Folkways](/wiki/Folkways_Records "Folkways Records")).

In 1957, [Glenn Yarbrough](/wiki/Glenn_Yarbrough "Glenn Yarbrough") recorded the song for [Elektra Records](/wiki/Elektra_Records "Elektra Records"). The song is also credited to [Ronnie Gilbert](/wiki/Ronnie_Gilbert "Ronnie Gilbert") on an album by [the Weavers](/wiki/The_Weavers "The Weavers") released in the late 1940s or early 1950s. [Pete Seeger](/wiki/Pete_Seeger "Pete Seeger") released a version on Folkways Records in 1958, which was re-released by Smithsonian Folkways in 2009.[[17]](#cite_note-folkwayslinernotes-17) [Andy Griffith](/wiki/Andy_Griffith "Andy Griffith") recorded the song on his 1959 album *Andy Griffith Shouts the Blues and Old Timey Songs*. The same year, a version by [Judy Collins](/wiki/Judy_Collins "Judy Collins") is released on the compilation *Folk Song Festival at Exodus*. In 1960, [Miriam Makeba](/wiki/Miriam_Makeba "Miriam Makeba") recorded the song on her [eponymous RCA album](/wiki/Miriam_Makeba_(album) "Miriam Makeba (album)").

[Joan Baez](/wiki/Joan_Baez "Joan Baez") recorded it in 1960 on her eponymous [debut album](/wiki/Joan_Baez_(album) "Joan Baez (album)"); she frequently performed the song in concert throughout her career. [Nina Simone](/wiki/Nina_Simone "Nina Simone") recorded her first version for the live album *[Nina at the Village Gate](/wiki/Nina_at_the_Village_Gate "Nina at the Village Gate")* in 1962. Simone later covered the song again on her 1967 studio album *[Nina Simone Sings the Blues](/wiki/Nina_Simone_Sings_the_Blues "Nina Simone Sings the Blues").* [Tim Hardin](/wiki/Tim_Hardin "Tim Hardin") sang it on *[This is Tim Hardin](/wiki/This_is_Tim_Hardin "This is Tim Hardin")*, recorded in 1964 but not released until 1967.[[30]](#cite_note-AM-30) [The Chambers Brothers](/wiki/The_Chambers_Brothers "The Chambers Brothers") recorded a version on *Feelin' the Blues*, released on Vault Records (1970).

### Van Ronk arrangement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Van Ronk arrangement")]

In late 1961, [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") recorded the song for his [debut album](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_(album) "Bob Dylan (album)"), released in March 1962. That release had no songwriting credit, but the liner notes indicate that Dylan learned this version of the song from [Dave Van Ronk](/wiki/Dave_Van_Ronk "Dave Van Ronk"). In an interview for the documentary *[No Direction Home](/wiki/No_Direction_Home "No Direction Home")*, Van Ronk said that he was intending to record the song and that Dylan copied his version. Van Ronk recorded it soon thereafter for the album *[Just Dave Van Ronk](/wiki/Just_Dave_Van_Ronk "Just Dave Van Ronk")*.

> I had learned it sometime in the 1950s, from a recording by [Hally Wood](/wiki/Hally_Wood "Hally Wood"), the Texas singer and collector, who had got it from an Alan Lomax field recording by a Kentucky woman named Georgia Turner. I put a different spin on it by altering the chords and using a bass line that descended in half steps—a common enough progression in jazz, but unusual among folksingers. By the early 1960s, the song had become one of my signature pieces, and I could hardly get off the stage without doing it.
> Then, one evening in 1962, I was sitting at my usual table in the back of the Kettle of Fish, and Dylan came slouching in. He had been up at the Columbia studios with [John Hammond](/wiki/John_Hammond_(producer) "John Hammond (producer)"), doing his first album. He was being very mysterioso about the whole thing, and nobody I knew had been to any of the sessions except [Suze](/wiki/Suze_Rotolo "Suze Rotolo"), his lady. I pumped him for information, but he was vague. Everything was going fine and, "Hey, would it be okay for me to record your arrangement of 'House of the Rising Sun?'" Oh, shit. "Jeez, Bobby, I'm going into the studio to do that myself in a few weeks. Can't it wait until your next album?" A long pause. "Uh-oh". I did not like the sound of that. "What exactly do you mean, 'Uh-oh'?" "Well", he said sheepishly, "I've already recorded it".[[31]](#cite_note-31)

An interview with [Eric Burdon](/wiki/Eric_Burdon "Eric Burdon") revealed that he first heard the song in a club in [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"), England, where it was sung by the [Northumbrian](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") folk singer [Johnny Handle](/wiki/Johnny_Handle "Johnny Handle"). [The Animals](/wiki/The_Animals "The Animals") were on tour with [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry") and chose it because they wanted something distinctive to sing.[[34]](#cite_note-BBC_Radio_4_program_18_Jan_2008-34)[[35]](#cite_note-pc29-35)

The Animals had begun featuring their arrangement of "The House of the Rising Sun" during a joint [concert tour](/wiki/Concert_tour "Concert tour") with Chuck Berry, using it as their closing number to differentiate themselves from acts that always closed with straight rockers.[[35]](#cite_note-pc29-35)[[36]](#cite_note-burdon1986-36) It elicited a tremendous reaction from the audience, convincing initially reluctant [producer](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer") [Mickie Most](/wiki/Mickie_Most "Mickie Most") that it had hit potential,[[36]](#cite_note-burdon1986-36) and between tour stops the group went to a small [recording studio](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio"), [De Lane Lea Studios](/wiki/De_Lane_Lea_Studios "De Lane Lea Studios") on [Kingsway](/wiki/Kingsway_(London) "Kingsway (London)") in [London](/wiki/London "London")[[36]](#cite_note-burdon1986-36) to capture it. The song was first performed in a concert in [Bellingham Town Hall](/wiki/Bellingham_Town_Hall "Bellingham Town Hall"), in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Recording and releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Recording and releases")]

The song was recorded in just one take on May 18, 1964,[[38]](#cite_note-chronicle-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) and it starts with a now-famous [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") [A minor chord](/wiki/Minor_chord "Minor chord") [arpeggio](/wiki/Arpeggio "Arpeggio") by [Hilton Valentine](/wiki/Hilton_Valentine "Hilton Valentine").[[1]](#cite_note-the_age-1)[[3]](#cite_note-mclean-3) According to Valentine, he simply took Dylan's chord sequence and played it as an arpeggio.[[40]](#cite_note-anthony-40) The performance takes off with Burdon's [lead vocal](/wiki/Lead_vocal "Lead vocal"), which has been variously described as "howling",[[2]](#cite_note-marsh1001-2) "soulful",[[41]](#cite_note-41) and as "...deep and gravelly as the north-east English [coal town](/wiki/Coal_town "Coal town") of [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") that spawned him".[[1]](#cite_note-the_age-1) Finally, [Alan Price](/wiki/Alan_Price "Alan Price")'s pulsating [organ](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)") part (played on a [Vox Continental](/wiki/Vox_Continental "Vox Continental")) completes the sound. Burdon later said, "We were looking for a song that would grab people's attention."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

As recorded, "The House of the Rising Sun" ran four and a half minutes, regarded as far too long for a pop single at the time.[[38]](#cite_note-chronicle-38) Producer Most, who initially did not really want to record the song at all,[[40]](#cite_note-anthony-40) said that on this occasion: "Everything was in the right place ... It only took 15 minutes to make so I can't take much credit for the production."[[43]](#cite_note-uk1000-43) He was nonetheless now a believer and declared it a single at its full length, saying "We're in a [microgroove](/wiki/LP_record "LP record") world now, we will release it."[[43]](#cite_note-uk1000-43)

In the U.S., the original single (MGM 13264) was a 2:58 version. The MGM Golden Circle reissue (KGC 179) featured the unedited 4:29 version, although the record label gives the edited playing time of 2:58. The edited version was included on the group's 1964 U.S. debut album *[The Animals](/wiki/The_Animals_(American_album) "The Animals (American album)")*, while the full version was later included on their best-selling 1966 U.S. greatest hits album, *[The Best of the Animals](/wiki/The_Best_of_The_Animals_(1966_album) "The Best of The Animals (1966 album)")*. The first American album release of the full version was on a 1965 compilation entitled *Mickie Most Presents British Go-Go* (MGM SE-4306), the cover of which, under the listing of "House of the Rising Sun", described it as the "Original uncut version". Americans could also hear the complete version in the movie *[Go Go Mania](/wiki/Pop_Gear "Pop Gear")* in the spring of 1965.

*[Cash Box](/wiki/Cash_Box "Cash Box")* described the U.S. single version as "a haunting, beat-ballad updating of the famed folk-blues opus that the group's lead delivers in telling solo vocal fashion."[[44]](#cite_note-cb-44)

"House of the Rising Sun" was not included on any of the group's British albums, but it was reissued as a single twice in subsequent decades, charting both times, reaching number 25 in 1972 and number 11 in 1982.

The Animals version was played in **6**  
**8** meter, unlike the **4**  
**4** of most earlier versions. Arranging credit went only to Alan Price. According to Burdon, this was simply because there was insufficient room to name all five band members on the record label, and Alan Price's first name was first alphabetically. This meant that only Price received songwriter's royalties for the hit, which has caused bitterness among the other band members ever since.[[3]](#cite_note-mclean-3)[[45]](#cite_note-45)
The song is in the public domain and Dave Van Ronk, who intended to sue them, realised that an arrangement cannot be protected by copyright.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Reception")]

"House of the Rising Sun" was a trans-Atlantic hit: after reaching the top of the [UK pop singles chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") in July 1964, it topped the [US pop singles chart](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") two months later, on September 5, 1964, where it stayed for three weeks. Many cite this as the first true classic rock song,[[46]](#cite_note-46) and it became the first [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion") number one unconnected with [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles").[[47]](#cite_note-47) It was the group's breakthrough hit in both countries and became their [signature song](/wiki/Signature_song "Signature song").[[48]](#cite_note-48) The song was also a hit in Ireland twice, peaking at No. 10 upon its initial release in 1964 and later reaching a brand new peak of No. 5 when reissued in 1982.

According to [John Steel](/wiki/John_Steel_(drummer) "John Steel (drummer)"), [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") told him that when he first heard the Animals' version on his car radio, he stopped to listen, "jumped out of his car" and "banged on the bonnet" (the hood of the car), inspiring him to [go electric](/wiki/Electric_Dylan_controversy "Electric Dylan controversy").[[49]](#cite_note-49) [Dave Van Ronk](/wiki/Dave_Van_Ronk "Dave Van Ronk") said that the Animals' version—like Dylan's version before it—was based on his arrangement of the song.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

[Dave Marsh](/wiki/Dave_Marsh "Dave Marsh") described the Animals' take on "The House of the Rising Sun" as "the first folk-rock hit", sounding "as if they'd connected the ancient tune to a live wire".[[2]](#cite_note-marsh1001-2) Writer Ralph McLean of the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") agreed that it was "arguably the first folk rock tune" and "a revolutionary single", after which "the face of modern music was changed forever."[[3]](#cite_note-mclean-3)

The Animals' rendition of the song is recognized as one of the classics of [British pop music](/wiki/British_pop_music "British pop music"). Writer [Lester Bangs](/wiki/Lester_Bangs "Lester Bangs") labeled it "a brilliant rearrangement" and "a new standard rendition of an old standard composition".[[51]](#cite_note-bangs-51) In 2021 (and 2024) it ranked number 471 on *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine's list of "[500 Greatest Songs of All Time](/wiki/500_Greatest_Songs_of_All_Time "500 Greatest Songs of All Time")". It is also one of the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame's "500 Songs That Shaped Rock and Roll"](/wiki/The_Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame%27s_500_Songs_that_Shaped_Rock_and_Roll "The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame's 500 Songs that Shaped Rock and Roll"). The [Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA)](/wiki/RIAA "RIAA") ranked it number 240 on their list of "[Songs of the Century](/wiki/Songs_of_the_Century "Songs of the Century")". In 1999, it received a [Grammy Hall of Fame Award](/wiki/List_of_Grammy_Hall_of_Fame_Award_recipients_E-I "List of Grammy Hall of Fame Award recipients E-I"). It has long since become a staple of [oldies](/wiki/Oldies "Oldies") and [classic rock](/wiki/Classic_rock "Classic rock") radio. A 2005 [Channel 5](/wiki/Channel_5_(British_TV_channel) "Channel 5 (British TV channel)") poll ranked it as Britain's fourth-favorite number one song.[[38]](#cite_note-chronicle-38)

In 1969, the Detroit band [Frijid Pink](/wiki/Frijid_Pink "Frijid Pink") recorded a psychedelic version of "House of the Rising Sun", which became an international hit in 1970. Their version is in **4**  
**4** (like Van Ronk's and most earlier versions, rather than the **6**  
**8** used by the Animals) and was driven by Gary Ray Thompson's [distorted guitar](/wiki/Distortion_(guitar) "Distortion (guitar)") with [fuzz](/wiki/Fuzz_guitar "Fuzz guitar") and [wah-wah](/wiki/Wah_wah_pedal "Wah wah pedal") effects, set against the frenetic [drumming](/wiki/Drum "Drum") of Richard Stevers.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

According to Stevers, the Frijid Pink recording of "House of the Rising Sun" was done impromptu when there was time left over at a recording session booked for the group at the Tera Shirma Recording Studios. Stevers later played snippets from that session's tracks for Paul Cannon, the music director of Detroit's premier rock radio station, [WKNR](/wiki/WDTW_(AM) "WDTW (AM)"); the two knew each other, as Cannon was the father of Stevers's girlfriend. Stevers recalled, "we went through the whole thing and [Cannon] didn't say much. Then 'House [of the Rising Sun]' started up and I immediately turned it off because it wasn't anything I really wanted him to hear." However, Cannon was intrigued and had Stevers play the complete track for him, then advising Stevers, "Tell [Parrot](/wiki/Parrot_Records "Parrot Records") [Frijid Pink's label] to drop "God Gave Me You" [the group's current single] and go with this one."[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Frijid Pink's "House of the Rising Sun" debuted at number 29 on the WKNR hit parade dated January 6, 1970, and broke nationally after some seven weeks—during which the track was re-serviced to radio three times—with a number 73 debut on the [Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") in *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* dated February 27, 1970 (number 97 Canada 1970/01/31), with a subsequent three-week ascent to the top 30 en route to a Hot 100 peak of number seven on April 4, 1970. The certification of the Frijid Pink single "House of the Rising Sun" as a [gold record](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") for domestic sales of one million units was reported in the issue of *Billboard* dated May 30, 1970.

The Frijid Pink single of "House of the Rising Sun" would give the song its most widespread international success, with top 10 status reached in Austria (number three), Belgium (Flemish region, number six), Canada (number three), Denmark (number three), Germany (two weeks at number one), Greece, Ireland (number seven), Israel (number four), the Netherlands (number three), Norway (seven weeks at number one), Poland (number two), Sweden (number six), Switzerland (number two), and the U.K. (number four). The single also charted in Australia (number 14), France (number 36), and Italy (number 54).

In August 1980, [Dolly Parton](/wiki/Dolly_Parton "Dolly Parton") released a cover of the song as the third single from her album *[9 to 5 and Odd Jobs](/wiki/9_to_5_and_Odd_Jobs "9 to 5 and Odd Jobs")*. Like Miller's earlier country hit, Parton's remake returns the song to its original lyric of being about a fallen woman. The Parton version makes it quite blunt, with a few new lyric lines that were written by Parton. Parton's remake reached number 14 on the U.S. country singles chart and crossed over to the pop charts, where it reached number 77 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"); it also reached number 30 on the U.S. [Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Adult_Contemporary_(chart) "Adult Contemporary (chart)") chart. Parton has occasionally performed the song live, including on her 1987–88 [television show](/wiki/Dolly_(1987_TV_series) "Dolly (1987 TV series)"), in an episode taped in New Orleans. In [Canada](/wiki/RPM_(magazine) "RPM (magazine)") it reached number 20.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

In 2014, [Five Finger Death Punch](/wiki/Five_Finger_Death_Punch "Five Finger Death Punch") released a cover version for their album *[The Wrong Side of Heaven and the Righteous Side of Hell, Volume 2](/wiki/The_Wrong_Side_of_Heaven_and_the_Righteous_Side_of_Hell,_Volume_2 "The Wrong Side of Heaven and the Righteous Side of Hell, Volume 2")*. Five Finger Death Punch's remake reached number 7 on the U.S. [Billboard Mainstream Rock](/wiki/Mainstream_Rock_(chart) "Mainstream Rock (chart)") chart. The setting is also changed in FFDP's version from [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") to [Sin City](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"), as a nod to the band's hometown, and is also well known for being a haven for gambling places like New Orleans (see also: [Gambling in the United States](/wiki/Gambling_in_the_United_States "Gambling in the United States")). The FFDP version is a cover of The Animals cover.

The song has been widely (more than 300 times[[105]](#cite_note-105)) [covered](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") and [remixed](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)") over the ages. Charting versions include:

* In 1969, [Claude King](/wiki/Claude_King "Claude King")'s version reached number 28 on [Canada](/wiki/RPM_(magazine) "RPM (magazine)")'s country charts, December 20, 1969.[[106]](#cite_note-106)
* In 1973, [Jody Miller](/wiki/Jody_Miller "Jody Miller")'s version reached number 29 on the country charts[[107]](#cite_note-107) and number 41 on the [Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Adult_Contemporary_(chart) "Adult Contemporary (chart)") chart.[[108]](#cite_note-108) In [Canada](/wiki/RPM_(magazine) "RPM (magazine)") it reached number 23 on the country charts and number 81 on the AC charts.[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)
* In 1977, [Santa Esmeralda](/wiki/Santa_Esmeralda "Santa Esmeralda") scored a top 20 disco hit with a dance version of the song and number 78 on the *Billboard* Hot 100.
* In 1996, [Gary Glitter](/wiki/Gary_Glitter "Gary Glitter") released a cover version of "House of the Rising Sun" as a single, which reached number 77 on the UK Singles Chart.

#### Los Speakers version (in Spanish)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Los Speakers version (in Spanish)")]

Colombian band [Los Speakers](/wiki/Los_Speakers "Los Speakers") covered the song under the title "La Casa del Sol Naciente", in their 1965 album of the same name.

#### Agnaldo Timóteo (in Portuguese)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Agnaldo Timóteo (in Portuguese)")]

The song is from [Agnaldo Timóteo](/wiki/Agnaldo_Tim%C3%B3teo "Agnaldo Timóteo")'s debut album, **Surge Um Astro**, released in 1965. The song was recorded and released in 1965, the year of Agnaldo Timóteo's rise to fame. The lyrics were adapted by Fred Jorge, who transformed the American blues into a ballad with a tone of melancholy and hope that deeply connected with the Brazilian audience.
[[114]](#cite_note-114)

#### EAV version and 'Wilbert Eckart und seine Volksmusik Stars' versions (in German)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: EAV version and 'Wilbert Eckart und seine Volksmusik Stars' versions (in German)")]

Two notable German covers/adaptions were created, one by [Erste Allgemeine Verunsicherung](/wiki/Erste_Allgemeine_Verunsicherung "Erste Allgemeine Verunsicherung"), which in 1989 recorded a song with lyrics telling the story of an [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") citizen fleeing East Berlin after the [Fall of the Berlin Wall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall") and his following disillusion with Western society.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Another one that gained international recognition was created for the soundtrack of *[Wolfenstein: The New Order](/wiki/Wolfenstein:_The_New_Order "Wolfenstein: The New Order")* in 2014, interpreting the song with [Volksmusik](/wiki/Volksmusik "Volksmusik") instrumentation, fitting the alternate future theme of the game in which [Nazi Germany won World War II](/wiki/Hypothetical_Axis_victory_in_World_War_II "Hypothetical Axis victory in World War II"), as part of a collection of 'adapted' pop hits.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

#### Miki Jevremović (in Serbo-Croatian)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Miki Jevremović (in Serbo-Croatian)")]

Famous Yugoslav singer Miodrag "Miki" Jevremović covered the song and included it in his 1964 EP "18 Žutih Ruža" (eng. "Eighteen Yellow Roses").

**Kult version (in Polish)**

Polish rock band [Kult](/wiki/Kult_(band) "Kult (band)") released a Polish-language version of the song under the title "Dom wschodzącego słońca" in the 1993 reedition of their self-titled debut album from 1987.

## Possible real locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_House_of_the_Rising_Sun&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Possible real locations")]

Various places in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans,_Louisiana "New Orleans, Louisiana") have been proposed as the inspiration for the song, with varying plausibility. The phrase "House of the Rising Sun" is often understood as a [euphemism](/wiki/Euphemism "Euphemism") for a [brothel](/wiki/Brothel "Brothel"), but it is uncertain as to whether the house described in the lyrics was an actual or a fictitious place. One theory is that the song is about a woman who killed her father, an alcoholic gambler who had beaten his wife. Therefore, the House of the Rising Sun may be a jailhouse, from which one would be the first person to see the sunrise (an idea supported by the lyric mentioning "a [ball and chain](/wiki/Ball_and_chain "Ball and chain")", though that phrase has been slang for marital relationships for at least as long as the song has been in print). Because women often sang the song, another theory is that the House of the Rising Sun was where prostitutes were detained while being treated for [syphilis](/wiki/Syphilis "Syphilis"). Since cures with mercury were ineffective, going back was very unlikely.[[7]](#cite_note-BBCh2g2-7)[[34]](#cite_note-BBC_Radio_4_program_18_Jan_2008-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Rising_Sun_New_Orleans_Picayune_1867.jpg/250px-Rising_Sun_New_Orleans_Picayune_1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rising_Sun_New_Orleans_Picayune_1867.jpg)

1867 advertisement noting the "Rising Sun Coffee House" building for rent or lease

Only three candidates that use the name *Rising Sun* have historical evidence—from old city directories and newspapers. The first was a small, short-lived [hotel](/wiki/Hotel "Hotel") on Conti Street in the [French Quarter](/wiki/French_Quarter "French Quarter") in the 1820s. It burned down in 1822. An excavation and document search in early 2005 found evidence that supported this claim, including an advertisement with language that may have euphemistically indicated prostitution. Archaeologists found an unusually large number of pots of rouge and cosmetics at the site.[[118]](#cite_note-nola01-118)

The second possibility was a "Rising Sun Hall" listed in late 19th-century city directories on what is now Cherokee Street, at the riverfront in the uptown [Carrollton neighborhood](/wiki/Carrollton,_Louisiana "Carrollton, Louisiana"), which seems to have been a building owned and used for meetings of a Social Aid and Pleasure Club, commonly rented out for dances and functions. It also is no longer extant. Definite links to gambling or prostitution (if any) are undocumented for either of these buildings.

A third was "The Rising Sun", which advertised in several local newspapers in the 1860s, located on what is now the lake side of the 100 block of [Decatur Street](/wiki/Decatur_Street_(New_Orleans) "Decatur Street (New Orleans)").[[119]](#cite_note-119) In various advertisements it is described as a "Restaurant", a "Lager Beer Salon", and a "Coffee House". At the time, New Orleans businesses listed as coffee houses often also sold alcoholic beverages.

Dave Van Ronk wrote in his biography *The Mayor of MacDougal Street* that at one time when he was in New Orleans someone approached him with a number of old photos of the city from the turn of the century. Among them "was a picture of a foreboding stone doorway with a carving on the lintel of a stylized rising sun ... It was the Orleans Parish women's prison".[[120]](#cite_note-120)

*Bizarre New Orleans*, a guidebook on New Orleans, asserts that the real house was at [1614 Esplanade Avenue](/wiki/Nola_Art_House "Nola Art House") between 1862 and 1874 and was said to have been named after its madam, Marianne LeSoleil Levant, whose surname means "the rising sun" in French.[[34]](#cite_note-BBC_Radio_4_program_18_Jan_2008-34)

Another guidebook, *Offbeat New Orleans*, asserts that the real House of the Rising Sun was at 826–830 St. Louis St. between 1862 and 1874, also purportedly named for Marianne LeSoleil Levant. The building still stands, and [Eric Burdon](/wiki/Eric_Burdon "Eric Burdon"), after visiting at the behest of the owner, said, "The house was talking to me".

There is a contemporary [B&B](/wiki/Bed_and_breakfast "Bed and breakfast") called the House of the Rising Sun, decorated in brothel style. The owners are fans of the song, but there is no connection with the original place.[[121]](#cite_note-ABC-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Not everyone is convinced that the house actually ever existed. Pamela D. Arceneaux, a research librarian at the Williams Research Center in New Orleans, is quoted as saying:

> I have made a study of the history of prostitution in New Orleans and have often confronted the perennial question, "Where is the House of the Rising Sun?" without finding a satisfactory answer. Although it is generally assumed that the singer is referring to a brothel, there is actually nothing in the lyrics that indicate that the "house" is a brothel. Many knowledgeable persons have conjectured that a better case can be made for either a gambling hall or a prison; however, to paraphrase Freud: sometimes lyrics are just lyrics.[[7]](#cite_note-BBCh2g2-7)
